topic: Continuous-outcome nonparametric sharp bounds on the proportion who benefit from treatment, Pr(Y1 > Y0), under a rank-concordance band -- Spearman's rho of the potential-outcome copula constrained to [rho_minus, rho_plus] rather than a fixed copula family -- with the critical concordance rho* at which the benefit-majority sign (Pr > 1/2 vs < 1/2) becomes data-undetermined; plug-in endpoint estimators and Imbens-Manski/Stoye inference over the partially-identified interval. Differentiate from arXiv:2605.11415 (ordinal latent-threshold, single-family envelope) and Fan-Park Makarov (marginals-only, copula free).

open_problems.open_problem: Derive sharp continuous-outcome bounds for Pr(Y1 > Y0) over all copulas with fixed potential-outcome marginals and Spearman's rho constrained to [rho_minus, rho_plus].

source_refs.claim: Fan and Park give sharp Makarov-style treatment-effect distribution bounds when dependence is unrestricted, and explicitly note dependence information could tighten them.
source_refs.author: Yanqin Fan and Sang Soo Park

source_refs.claim: He and Li identify ordinal probability-of-benefit estimands by fixing a parametric copula association parameter, not by optimizing over a nonparametric rho band.
source_refs.author: Peiyu He and Fan Li

source_refs.claim: Zhang and Richardson revisit Makarov/Kolmogorov bounds for individual treatment effects and correct sharpness gaps, reinforcing that constrained-coupling sharpness is delicate.
source_refs.author: Zhehao Zhang and Thomas S. Richardson

source_refs.excerpt: It does not by itself prove sharpness
lower and upper endpoints ... are attained by linear programs over this polytope

open_problems.why_unsolved: The published literature covers two adjacent corners: Fan-Park/Makarov solve the copula-free marginal-only case, while He-Li solve a single-family copula sensitivity curve for ordinal outcomes. Prior CausalSmith work reached fixed-rho GPN envelope algebra and finite rank-LP templates, but explicitly left sharpness and the benefit event unresolved. The missing obstacle is an infinite-dimensional constrained-copula problem with a global linear rank-moment restriction, where the objective is the half-plane event Q1(U) > Q0(V), not a full CDF point or a named copula family.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A primal-dual sharp-identification theorem for infimum and supremum of integral 1{Q1(u) > Q0(v)} dC(u,v) over copulas C with uniform marginals and rho(C) in [rho_minus, rho_plus], including extremal-copula attainability or a computable sharp endpoint algorithm.

writing_exemplar.why: It is the closest motivation arc for probability of benefit plus copula sensitivity, but the proposal should pivot from single-family ordinal sensitivity to continuous nonparametric rho-band sharp bounds.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: probability of benefit is unidentified -> fixed copulas make it identified but family-dependent -> rho-band sharp bounds keep sensitivity interpretable without family commitment

method_exemplars.technique: Makarov/Williamson-Downs endpoint construction and sharpness proof for treatment-effect distribution bounds from identified marginals.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The benefit probability is the zero-threshold treatment-effect CDF complement, now with an added Spearman-rho feasibility constraint.

method_exemplars.technique: Kantorovich duality for transport/coupling optimization with marginal constraints, adapted by adding a linear rank-moment constraint.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The desired endpoint is a constrained optimal-transport value over couplings with known marginals.

method_exemplars.technique: Plug-in nonparametric endpoint estimation plus fewer-than-n bootstrap for nonregular sharp bound estimators.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It supplies the direct estimation layer once the Spearman-band endpoint map is characterized.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize the critical concordance set rho* at which the sign of the benefit majority, Pr(Y1 > Y0) > 1/2 versus < 1/2, becomes data-undetermined under a Spearman-rho copula band.

source_refs.claim: Mullahy frames inequality probabilities, including one potential outcome exceeding another, as policy-relevant parameters boundable from marginals.
source_refs.author: John Mullahy
source_refs.venue: Journal of Health Economics / NBER Working Paper

source_refs.claim: Firpo and Ridder show that pointwise Makarov bounds are not uniformly sharp for functionals, motivating direct analysis of benefit-sign functionals.
source_refs.author: Sergio Firpo and Geert Ridder

source_refs.excerpt: there is a critical radius rho^star in (0,1)
increasing rho unambiguously means increasing L_GPN

open_problems.why_unsolved: Mullahy motivates inequality probabilities as policy signs and Firpo-Ridder warn against deriving functional conclusions from pointwise CDF bounds, but neither gives a concordance threshold. Prior inverse-threshold proposals target continuous GPN or adjacent-rank slope signs, and one reviewer explicitly asked for rho-orientation discipline. For benefit probability, a scalar Spearman band may not monotonically order the event Q1(U) > Q0(V); the threshold can be an interval, empty, or nonunique unless additional quantile-crossing conditions are imposed.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A theorem defining rho*_L and rho*_U, or a critical rho-set, with no-crossing, already-crossed, tie, and nonmonotone cases, plus sufficient conditions under which the lower and upper benefit endpoints cross the 1/2 boundary monotonically.

writing_exemplar.why: It motivates inequality probabilities as directly interpretable majority-benefit quantities for applied decisions.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: average effects miss incidence -> inequality probabilities answer who benefits -> rho* says when concordance assumptions determine the benefit-majority sign

method_exemplars.technique: Direct functional bounding rather than reading a policy functional from pointwise CDF envelopes.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The majority sign is a decision functional of an identified interval, not merely a point on the treatment-effect CDF.

method_exemplars.technique: Precision-corrected inference for extrema and threshold decisions generated by estimated bound functions.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The rho* object is an inverse crossing of estimated lower and upper bound curves.

open_problems.open_problem: Develop plug-in endpoint estimators and uniformly valid confidence intervals for the Spearman-band identified interval for Pr(Y1 > Y0), including boundary regimes where the interval straddles 1/2.

source_refs.claim: Fan and Park derive nonparametric estimators and inference for sharp Makarov bounds, noting standard bootstrap failure and using fewer-than-n bootstrap.
source_refs.author: Yanqin Fan and Sang Soo Park

source_refs.claim: Imbens and Manski give confidence intervals for scalar interval-identified parameters with estimated lower and upper bounds.
source_refs.author: Guido W. Imbens and Charles F. Manski

source_refs.claim: Stoye extends Imbens-Manski interval inference and pinpoints nuisance preestimation and uniformity issues.
source_refs.author: Joerg Stoye

source_refs.excerpt: correctly refutes the proposed one-sided CI claim
future work here needs a genuinely new correction/calibration rule

open_problems.why_unsolved: Fan-Park inference covers Makarov endpoints without a Spearman-band constraint, and Imbens-Manski/Stoye provide generic interval-ID coverage logic once endpoint limit theory is available. Prior CausalSmith reviewers rejected a naive one-sided inversion and warned that generic bootstrap/max arguments are already known unless tied to a new calibration rule. Here the endpoint estimators are extrema of an empirical infinite-dimensional LP with estimated quantiles and a rank-moment constraint; active-set changes, flat crossings, and near-zero interval width make ordinary delta-method or same-n bootstrap arguments nonautomatic.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A limit law or directional-Hadamard differentiability theorem for the lower and upper Spearman-band endpoint maps, a valid bootstrap/subsampling or calibrated-projection procedure, and an Imbens-Manski/Stoye-style confidence interval with uniform coverage over short, wide, and 1/2-straddling identified sets.

writing_exemplar.why: It gives the clean interval-identification motivation arc that downstream inference should mirror.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: identified set is an interval -> endpoint estimators have sampling error -> valid inference must cover the true value uniformly over interval widths

method_exemplars.technique: Endpoint plug-in asymptotics and fewer-than-n bootstrap for sharp distributional treatment-effect bounds.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It is the direct ancestor for estimating treatment-effect bound endpoints from marginal outcome distributions.

method_exemplars.technique: Uniformly valid refinements for confidence intervals when interval width and nuisance preestimation create nonregularity.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The Spearman-band interval can be short, wide, or near the majority threshold, exactly the regimes where uniformity matters.

method_exemplars.technique: Bootstrap calibrated projection over relaxed sample inequalities for partially identified functionals.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The rho* sign-threshold can be posed as a projection/inversion of endpoint inequalities.

open_problems.open_problem: Determine whether conditional Spearman-band benefit bounds can be safely pooled over covariates, or whether sharpness requires retaining conditional-cell dependence budgets.

source_refs.claim: Fan, Guerre, and Zhu characterize identified sets for joint-potential-outcome functionals and note that covariates can shrink identified sets.
source_refs.author: Yanqin Fan, Emmanuel Guerre and Daming Zhu

source_refs.claim: Frandsen, Lefgren, and Leslie improve DTE bounds using testable dependence restrictions such as mutual stochastic increasingness.
source_refs.author: Brigham R. Frandsen, Lars J. Lefgren and Emily C. Leslie

source_refs.claim: He and Li work under unconfoundedness with nuisance estimation for copula-based ordinal benefit estimands.
source_refs.author: Peiyu He and Fan Li

source_refs.excerpt: pooled report differs from the conditional sharp support value
No claim is made here about continuous outcomes ... or additional dependence restrictions

open_problems.why_unsolved: Conditional marginals are identifiable in randomized or unconfounded designs, and existing work shows covariate conditioning can tighten joint-potential-outcome bounds. But a Spearman-rho restriction may be elicited as a global rank-concordance budget rather than a fixed stratum-by-stratum band. The prior OT-DTE aggregation proposal found a pooled-versus-conditional support-function defect but explicitly avoided continuous outcomes and added dependence restrictions, so it does not settle whether integrated conditional rho-band bounds equal the sharp global-rho feasible set.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A decomposition theorem, or counterexample, comparing three identified sets: integrated conditional rho-band bounds, unconditional pooled rho-band bounds, and sharp bounds with a global Spearman-rho budget allocated across covariate cells.

writing_exemplar.why: It frames how covariates enter partial identification of joint-potential-outcome functionals.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: marginals identify only a set of latent joint laws -> covariates can shrink that set -> global concordance budgets may not decompose by cell

method_exemplars.technique: Covariate-conditional identified-set characterization for functionals of the joint potential-outcome distribution.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The target is a covariate version of a joint-potential-outcome functional with an extra dependence constraint.

method_exemplars.technique: Use of testable nonparametric dependence restrictions to sharpen DTE bounds.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It is the closest econometric model where dependence restrictions tighten treatment-effect distribution bounds.

method_exemplars.technique: Inference for supremum/infimum bounds over rich index sets using precision correction.
method_exemplars.why_closest: Conditional-cell rho budgets generate intersection or support-function extrema over covariate indices.

open_problems.open_problem: Extend Spearman-band benefit bounds to mixed, discrete, and thresholded outcomes, clarifying when ordinal latent-threshold analyses approximate or fail to approximate the continuous-outcome problem.

source_refs.claim: Zhang and Richardson show that discontinuous marginals create subtle unresolved sharpness and logical-gap issues in Makarov treatment-effect applications.
source_refs.author: Zhehao Zhang and Thomas S. Richardson

source_refs.claim: Huang, Fang, Hanley, and Rosenblum construct confidence intervals for the fraction who benefit for ordinal or binary outcomes, allowing user-defined assumptions.
source_refs.author: Emily J. Huang, Ethan X. Fang, Daniel F. Hanley and Michael Rosenblum

source_refs.claim: de Aguas et al. define probability of tiered benefit for ordered categorical or thresholded continuous outcomes and emphasize nonregular inference for sharp bounds.
source_refs.author: Johan de Aguas, Sebastian Krumscheid, Johan Pensar and Guido Biele

source_refs.excerpt: ordinal post-treatment intermediate, Y in finite support Ygrid
It does not propose ... a new copula family

open_problems.why_unsolved: Spearman's rho is a clean copula functional for continuous marginals, but atoms and ties make ranks and copulas nonunique. Ordinal and tiered-benefit papers solve finite-grid or thresholded versions, while the user-specified target is continuous and nonparametric in the copula family. The open obstacle is to decide whether finite ordinal LP envelopes are approximations to the continuous rho-band problem, conservative surrogates, or different objects once latent thresholds and tie conventions are introduced.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A mixed-outcome identification theorem specifying the correct rank-concordance functional with ties, proving convergence or giving counterexamples between latent-continuous and observed-ordinal bounds, and stating conditions under which threshold coarsening preserves the benefit-majority sign.

writing_exemplar.why: It is a good template for exposing a subtle foundational gap in a classical bounds literature.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: classical bounds look settled -> discontinuities expose hidden sharpness issues -> treatment-effect conclusions need corrected foundations

method_exemplars.technique: Quadratic-program and stochastic-optimization inference for finite-support fraction-who-benefit bounds.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It is the closest finite-support probability-of-benefit inference problem.

method_exemplars.technique: Sharpness audit for discontinuous-marginal Makarov-type problems.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It directly flags atom and discontinuity hazards that Spearman-band benefit bounds must handle.

open_problems.open_problem: Prove or refute measurable extremal-copula pasting for continuous conditional marginals under a global Spearman-rho band.

source_refs.excerpt: Sharpness needs a measurable conditional pasting argument
load-bearing pastability lemma asserted not derived

open_problems.why_unsolved: Several prior CausalSmith attempts relied on local or conditional extremal couplings but flagged the pastability lemma as asserted rather than derived. A Spearman-rho band is an integral rank-moment constraint, so independently choosing pointwise or conditional extremal copulas may violate the global concordance budget after integration over covariates or over quantile regions. This is a sharpness obstacle, not a cosmetic proof gap.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A measurable-selection and pasting theorem for rank-moment-constrained copulas that preserves the global Spearman budget, or a counterexample showing that conditional sharp endpoints cannot be jointly pasted into a globally feasible latent law.

writing_exemplar.why: It frames sharp partial identification around extension and completion of subdistribution-like objects.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: local restrictions imply partial laws -> sharpness requires a global extension -> rho-band benefit bounds need a feasible extremal copula completion

method_exemplars.technique: Extendibility of partial distribution objects to proper distributions as a sharpness proof device.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The open problem asks whether local extremal copulas can be extended to a global feasible copula under a rank-moment restriction.

method_exemplars.technique: Duality and compactness for coupling problems with marginal constraints.
method_exemplars.why_closest: A counterexample or positive pasting theorem will likely be expressed as feasible/infeasible constrained couplings.

method_exemplars.technique: Strong-approximation inference for bounds defined by extrema over large index classes.
method_exemplars.why_closest: If pasting holds through a conditional-index representation, inference will require uniform control over the pasted endpoint class.

literature_map:
1. Foundational marginal-only bounds start with Makarov, Rueschendorf, Frank-Nelsen-Schweizer, and Williamson-Downs on sums/differences with fixed marginals.
2. Fan-Park 2010 make Makarov treatment-effect CDF bounds operational and study nonparametric inference; their conclusion explicitly points to dependence-information tightening as remaining work.
3. Firpo-Ridder 2019 show pointwise Makarov bounds are not uniformly sharp for functionals, so benefit-probability and majority-sign questions should be bounded directly.
4. Fan-Guerre-Zhu 2017 and related work characterize broader joint-potential-outcome functionals and the role of covariates in shrinking identified sets.
5. Frandsen-Lefgren-Leslie 2021 is the main econometric bridge from marginal DTE bounds to nonparametric dependence restrictions with testable implications.
6. Mullahy 2018 frames inequality probabilities as applied treatment-effect parameters; Huang-Fang-Hanley-Rosenblum 2019 and de Aguas et al. 2025 focus on ordinal/tiered fraction-who-benefit inference.
7. He-Li 2026 is the direct contrast: ordinal outcomes plus parametric copula sensitivity curves, not continuous outcomes plus sharp nonparametric Spearman-band bounds.
8. Zhang-Richardson 2025 revisit Kolmogorov/Makarov sharpness and flag discontinuous-marginal hazards relevant to mixed or ordinal extensions.
9. Copula theory supplies Spearman's rho as a rank/concordance functional of the copula; the hard step is optimizing a benefit-event functional over all copulas satisfying a rho band.
10. Optimal transport and coupling duality are the natural method substrate for constrained endpoint formulas and dual certificates.
11. Imbens-Manski 2004, Stoye 2009, Kaido-Molinari-Stoye 2019, and Chernozhukov-Lee-Rosen 2013 are the inference backbone once endpoint limit theory exists.
12. The scanned literature leaves open continuous-outcome sharp bounds for Pr(Y1>Y0) under a Spearman-rho band, rho* majority-threshold geometry, endpoint inference, covariate pooling, and mixed-outcome approximation.
13. The appended study-pipeline gaps on nonparametric bound intersections and Imbens-Manski/Stoye interval inference reinforce the stat entry; LATE and interval-regression study gaps were off-topic for this qid.
14. No web source was found that already gives the requested continuous rank-concordance-band benefit interval or its majority-sign critical rho.
prior_proposal_map:
No exact prior proposal for pid_benefit_concordance_band was present; the active qid directory only had state/pipeline scaffolding.
pid_gpn_continuous_rho is the closest direct ancestor: inverse rho_star for continuous GPN, fixed-rho copula-envelope algebra, explicit sharpness caveats, and a refuted naive one-sided CI.
pid_dose_response_iv supplies the reusable finite rank-copula LP pattern and critical-rho phase language, but its target is adjacent dose-response slope, not Pr(Y1>Y0).
pid_ot_dte_marginal is the main Fan-Park/Makarov and OT-pasting warning source: local or pointwise DTE bounds can fail to paste into one sharp joint law, and pooled reports can differ from conditional support values.
pid_bound_intersection_inference is useful as a warning that generic active-set or bootstrap claims were rejected unless a new calibrated correction rule is supplied.
pid_ordinal_principal_strata and ordinal bank entries are adjacent only as finite ordinal LP contrasts; they help differentiate from arXiv:2605.11415 but are not the main model.
Reviewer JSON strengthening_paths yielded two high-value prior-only signals: rho orientation must be explicit, and LP active-constraint thresholds need certificates.
Study-pipeline gaps on inference for bound intersections and joint restrictions were retained as supporting context; LATE/defier and interval-regression gaps were filtered out as topic-mismatched.
